game_name: Wizards & Warriors III: Kuros: Visions of Power

design_philosophy.core_loop: Explore three interconnected realms (town, underworld, palace), collect class-specific statues, return them to guilds to unlock new abilities and disguises (knight/thief/wizard), use unlocked powers to access previously unreachable areas and progress toward confronting Malkil
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Progression gated by non-linear ability acquisition; player must discover which statue unlocks which guild test, creating exploration-based puzzle tension rather than combat tension
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Weak hero (stripped of armor and power) gradually rebuilding strength through cunning disguises and learned abilities, operating undetected within an occupied city while preparing for final confrontation
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Deliberately eschews continues/passwords (unlike predecessors), forcing permadeath stakes; emphasizes non-linear exploration and ability-gating over combat; homage to Atic Atac with open-ended progression; prioritizes bold graphics and expansive gameplay over traditional action sequences

difficulty.lives_system: Three lives at game start; game ends when all lives lost
difficulty.continues_system: No continue feature (departure from previous Wizards & Warriors games)
difficulty.checkpointing: No password/checkpoint system; no mid-game saves
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Limited; permadeath structure with no continues creates high stakes, but difficulty curve is fixed. Guild tests scale in difficulty as player progresses through bronze/silver/gold levels of each class.

progression.upgrade_system: Class-based ability tree: three character classes (knight/thief/wizard) each with three progression tiers (bronze/silver/gold). Each tier grants new disguise, attire, and ability that unlocks new areas. Knight: improved weaponry; Wizard: greater magic; Thief: increased access to buildings via doors/windows
progression.unlock_mechanism: Statue collection and guild tests; find class statue → return to corresponding guild → defeat boss-based test → unlock new ability/area access. Four secret gems collected from: three rescued princesses + defeating dragon in underworld. Gems unlock hidden passage to castle throne room
replayability_hooks: Non-linear exploration allows varied discovery order
Secret gems and hidden princess locations encourage thorough exploration
No continues or passwords increase challenge value for skilled players

world_building_techniques: Direct sequel narrative continuation from Ironsword (Malkil's spirit survives)
Environmental storytelling through city occupation and kingdom disruption (Good King James dethroned)
Character agency through player-driven disguise adoption (infiltration gameplay reflects narrative role)
NPC interaction via shopkeeper information purchases
Damsel rescue subquests tied to gem collection (three princesses)

boss_design.philosophy: Guild-based progression bosses serve as ability gate-keepers; boss defeats grant class tier advancement and corresponding power increases. Each guild (knight/thief/wizard) has three progressively difficult boss encounters tied to statue tiers
notable_bosses: Guild bosses (knight, thief, wizard guilds - one per tier, three tiers each)
Dragon boss in town underworld (required for final gem)
Malkil - final encounter in castle throne room

faction_indicators: Three character classes represent distinct power factions (Knight/Thief/Wizard guilds)
Good King James (dethroned authority) vs Malkil's occupying force
Three princesses as faction/loyalty markers (rescue = allegiance establishment)
danger_signals: City occupation and throne seizure by dark wizard
Kuros stripped of armor, memory, and honor (societal debasement)
Disguise requirement for safe city passage (occupation surveillance)
environmental_storytelling: Piedup city layout divided into three realms (town/underworld/palace) reflecting hierarchy and control
Inns and shops throughout city suggest civilian infrastructure under occupation
Guild halls represent organized power structures and class distinctions
Statue placement in world encodes class hierarchy and progression pathway
